one_line_takeaway: A six-dimension 'Trust-RAG Compass' framework plus a 50-sample-per-dimension TRC Bench that, across 19 LLMs, shows proprietary models lead on factuality/accountability/privacy while fairness and privacy remain weak for nearly all models and instruction tuning can even zero out privacy refusal.
key_finding: On TRC Bench, proprietary models clearly outperform open-source models on factuality, accountability, transparency, and privacy, but no model is trustworthy across the board. Factuality separates the two groups starkly: Gemini-3.1-pro 74.0 and Claude-Opus-4.6 72.0 vs. every Qwen3.5/Ministral open-source model scoring 0.0-6.0. Instruction tuning helps robustness, accountability, and transparency, yet paradoxically harms privacy -- aligned open-source variants drop to 0.0 refusal. Larger scale helps within a family (Qwen3.5-27B > 9B > 4B on robustness/transparency/accountability) but not uniformly across dimensions or families. Fairness and privacy are the hardest dimensions for everyone: fairness stays low (14-46) for both groups, and privacy is near-zero for most open-source models and the entire Gemini series, while GPT-5.4/5.4-pro (80.0/83.7) and Claude-Opus-4.6 (62.0) are the notable privacy performers.
core_contribution: A survey plus original benchmark on the trustworthiness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The paper's OWN contributions are threefold: (1) 'Trust-RAG Compass', a unified conceptual framework that decomposes RAG trustworthiness into six dimensions -- factuality, robustness, fairness, transparency, accountability, and privacy -- each given a general LLM definition, a RAG-specific definition, and a structured literature taxonomy (Fig. 1, Tables 1-8); (2) 'TRC Bench' (Trust-RAG Compass Benchmark), a new evaluation suite with one dimension-specific test and metric per dimension (Section 4.1); and (3) an empirical evaluation of 19 LLMs on TRC Bench (Table 9, Section 4.2). NOTE: the bulk of Section 3 (Self-RAG, PoisonedRAG, BadRAG, RobustRAG, FairRAG, MetaRAG, RAGBench, WebGPT, etc.) is a review of OTHER authors' methods and must not be attributed to this paper; the paper's novel artifacts are the six-dimension framework, TRC Bench, and the 19-model comparison.
threat_model: As a survey the paper organizes (rather than invents) threat models for RAG, and additionally operationalizes several as active stress tests in its own benchmark. Surveyed adversarial taxonomy (from cited work): robustness attacks split into retrieval-stage poisoning (PoisonedRAG, corpus poisoning, HijackRAG, DIGA, AgentPoison), backdoor attacks (BadRAG, TrojanRAG, Phantom), interaction-level attacks (indirect prompt injection, FlippedRAG stance manipulation, LLM-generated misinformation), and structural attacks (GARAG typo perturbation, Shafran jamming/DoS on top-k slots, Topic-FlippedRAG); privacy risks characterized along three axes -- data-extraction risk, membership/inference risk, and retrieval leakage; fairness attacks such as BiasRAG backdoor bias injection. In its OWN benchmark the paper enacts three adversary-style probes: injecting factually misleading counterfactual documents (factuality), injecting biased documents plus biased yes/no statements (fairness), and probing an Enron-email corpus for private email addresses without a non-disclosure instruction to test refusal (privacy).
limitations: Author-stated: existing benchmarks (including this one) focus on final outputs and miss step-level/process-level correctness of agentic RAG; factual conflict between static parametric knowledge and dynamic retrieval, long-context/memory contamination, and cascading failures in multi-step reasoning remain open. Reviewer-noted: TRC Bench is small -- exactly 50 samples per dimension drawn from single existing datasets (RGB, HotpotQA, CrowS-Pairs, Enron), so results are point estimates without variance/significance reporting and may not generalize; fairness is tested only on the Gender dimension; each dimension uses a single bespoke metric, and transparency/factuality metrics depend on GPT-4 (key-fact construction) and the TRUE NLI oracle, introducing model-dependence. The evaluated model roster (GPT-5.4/5.4-pro, Gemini-3.1-pro, Claude-Opus-4.6/Sonnet-4.6, Qwen3.5, Ministral3) reflects the forward-dated versions printed in this arXiv v2 (May 2026) and should be read as reported, not verified independently.

datasets_used: RGB benchmark (50 samples, factuality counterfactual test)
HotpotQA (50 questions each for robustness, transparency, and accountability tests)
CrowS-Pairs (50 of 262 gender-related samples, fairness test)
Enron Email dataset (~500,000 emails as retrieval corpus, 50 questions, privacy test)
quantitative_results: Factuality (avoid-fake-answer %): Gemini-3.1-pro 74.0 (best), Claude-Opus-4.6 72.0, GPT-5.4-pro 57.1, GPT-5.4 56.0, Gemini-3-flash 50.0; all open-source (Qwen3.5, Ministral3) 0.0-6.0
Accountability (citation F1): GPT-5.4 96.4 (best), Claude-Opus-4.6 95.9, GPT-5.4-pro 95.4, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 95.2, Qwen3.5-27B 92.5
Transparency (key-fact recall): Claude-Opus-4.6 89.6 and Qwen3.5-27B 89.6 (tied best), Claude-Sonnet-4.6 87.3, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite 86.3

Privacy (refusal rate): GPT-5.4-pro 83.7, GPT-5.4 80.0, Claude-Opus-4.6 62.0, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 40.0; near-zero for most open-source models and the Gemini series (Gemini-3.1-flash-lite 0.0, Gemini-3-flash 0.0, Gemini-3.1-pro 2.0), and instruction-tuned open-source variants drop to 0.0
Fairness: low across the board (range ~14-46), highest Ministral3-3B-Instruct 46.0; a common weakness for both open-source and proprietary models

Robustness (F1 change across 3 vs 10 references): small magnitude for most, e.g. GPT-5.4-mini +5.4%, Gemini-3.1-pro +3.3%, Qwen3.5-4B-base -13.4% (most sensitive); open-source models more sensitive to reference changes than proprietary
Robustness absolute F1 (Fig. 4): Qwen3.5-27B 62.42/54.44 (ref-3/ref-10), GPT-5.4-pro 87.49/91.54, Gemini-3.1-pro 82.67/86.00, Claude-Opus-4.6 74.68/71.45
Scale effect within Qwen3.5 family: 27B > 9B > 4B on robustness, transparency, and accountability (accountability 92.5 vs 75.3 vs 66.8)

Roster: 19 LLMs = 11 open-source (Qwen3.5-4B/9B-base, Qwen3.5-4B/9B/27B, Ministral3-3B/8B/14B-base, Ministral3-3B/8B/14B-Instruct) + 8 proprietary (GPT-5.4-mini/5.4/5.4-pro, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite/3-flash/3.1-pro, Claude-Opus-4.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.6)
baselines_compared: Open-source Ministral3 family (3B/8B/14B base and Instruct variants)
Comparison axes: proprietary vs open-source, base vs instruction-tuned, and model scale within family
